quotes.verbatim_quote:
These shapes contain both drop 2 and drop 3 chords in root position, which is the best
place to get started playing jazz guitar chords.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the voicing system (drop 2 / drop 3 in root position) that underpins all the chord shapes introduced in this chapter.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
You will learn smaller shapes in the next section, which are easier to play, but these
four-note shapes drill the sound of jazz into your ears better than the smaller shapes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Gives a pedagogical rule about why full four-note voicings are prioritized first — ear training rationale over ease of execution.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Start by learning one shape for each chord type then take those changes to a jazz tune
you know or are working on.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the method's prescribed practice sequence: isolate one shape per chord quality, then immediately apply it in a musical context.
Defines the dual naming convention — 'Minor 7b5' and 'Half-Diminished' — that students need to recognise in lead sheets and chord charts.
